title: Conjecture 1.2 (Heckman and Thomas)
statement_text: Every subcubic triangle-free planar graph is fractionally $8/3$-colorable.
context_text: Heckman and Thomas proved that every $n$-vertex planar subcubic triangle-free graph has an independent set of size at least $3n/8$ and proposed this corresponding fractional chromatic number strengthening. The present paper obtains the weaker bound of $11/4$ for all such graphs as a partial step toward the conjecture.
paper_contribution: Proves that every subcubic triangle-free planar graph is fractionally $11/4$-colorable (Corollary 1.5), since the two critical cubic exceptions $F^{(1)}_{14}$ and $F^{(2)}_{14}$ are both non-planar.
notes: PDF source — math notation is clean and unambiguous.

title: Conjecture 1.3 (Cames van Batenburg et al.)
statement_text: Every subcubic triangle-free graph avoiding $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F_{11}$, $F_{22}$, $F^{(1)}_{19}$, and $F^{(2)}_{19}$ as subgraphs is fractionally $8/3$-colorable.
context_text: Cames van Batenburg et al. proposed this conjecture as a natural fractional-coloring strengthening of their confirmation of the Fraughnaugh–Locke independence number conjecture; it would also imply Conjecture 1.2 of Heckman and Thomas. The present paper explicitly describes its main theorem as the first step towards this conjecture.
paper_contribution: Proves the first step: every subcubic triangle-free graph with no component isomorphic to $F^{(1)}_{14}$ or $F^{(2)}_{14}$ has fractional chromatic number at most $11/4$ (Theorem 1.4).
notes: PDF source — superscript/subscript notation for $F^{(1)}_{14}$ etc. reconstructed from unambiguous context.

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: Every subcubic triangle-free graph avoiding $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F_{11}$, and $F_{22}$ as subgraphs is fractionally $19/7$-colorable.
context_text: The authors pose this as an intermediate conjecture between their main result (Theorem 1.4, giving $11/4$-colorability when only $F^{(1)}_{14}$ and $F^{(2)}_{14}$ are excluded) and Conjecture 1.3 (giving $8/3$-colorability when six graphs are excluded). It is noted that $F_{11}$ and $F_{22}$ both have fractional chromatic number exactly $11/4$, while $F^{(1)}_{19}$ and $F^{(2)}_{19}$ have fractional chromatic number $19/7$, motivating this intermediate bound.
notes: PDF source — the bound $19/7$ and the excluded subgraphs are stated clearly in the text.
